publisher: Cuba Study Group / Observatory on Cuban Economics
title: Cuba's Structural Crisis and the Path to Transformation
synopsis: Independent Cuban economists argue the crisis is the product of decades of accumulated structural decay — not a passing shock or solely the U.S. embargo. They benchmark Cuba against its neighbors and lay out recovery priorities: energy, agriculture, logistics, and tourism, plus social protection and institutional rebuild.
key_stat: Cuba GDP/capita (PPP) ~$6,300 vs. Dominican Republic ~$28,747; inflation 31.8%.
why_matters: The analytical backbone of the "atlas now, invest when it opens" thesis — and it names the exact sectors our pins cover.

publisher: Cuba Study Group / Observatory on Cuban Economics
title: Stopping the Freefall: Cuba's Emergency Stabilization Phase
synopsis: Vidal proposes a staged roadmap that begins with emergency stabilization to halt the collapse, then sequences deeper reform. He flags the private sector, the diaspora, and a possible easing of sanctions as the three levers most likely to power the initial recovery.
key_stat: GDP has contracted >23% since 2019 (with further 2026 declines projected).
why_matters: Diaspora + private sector + payment rails = the QvaPay / Upside Hubs thesis, validated by an independent Cuban economist.

publisher: Cuba Study Group / Observatory on Cuban Economics
title: A Social Market Economy Requires Democratic Rule of Law
synopsis: The authors argue recovery needs more than economics — it needs a social-market economy under democratic rule of law, with protected private property, competition, and legal guarantees, recognizing all forms of ownership equally.
why_matters: Property-rights reform is the precondition that resolves our Helms-Burton / rightful-owners layer — clean title becomes investable title.

publisher: Cuba Study Group / Observatory on Cuban Economics
title: Guiding Principles for Cuba's Economic Transformation
synopsis: "Stabilize to gain time, reform to grow." Everleny stresses rebuilding supply and productivity, stronger legal certainty, and institutional modernization — and notes that private-sector imports are already easing shortages on the ground.
why_matters: Confirms that private imports / payments (our rails) are already a working channel — not theoretical.

publisher: Cuba Study Group / Observatory on Cuban Economics
title: Stabilizing the Cuban Economy: How Should Priorities Be Defined?
synopsis: The authors offer nine criteria for sequencing reforms — severity, institutional capacity, ability to mobilize financing, infrastructure readiness, welfare impact, and international precedent — to identify measures that produce fast results. Lifting restrictions on private enterprise ranks among the highest-leverage early moves.
why_matters: Gives us a rubric to rank which Atlas opportunities turn real first.
